statement.markdown: Given a string containing just the characters '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. The brackets must close in the correct order, i.e., '()' and '()[]{}' are all valid but '(]' and '([)]' are not.

examples.explanation: The input string is not valid.
The input string is not valid.

hints.text: Consider using a data structure to keep track of opening brackets and their corresponding closing brackets.
You can use a stack to store the opening brackets and then check if the closing bracket matches the top of the stack.
When pushing an opening bracket onto the stack, you can assign a value to it that corresponds to its closing bracket.
For example, you can assign '}' to '(', ']' to '[', and ')' to '(', and then check if the top of the stack matches the closing bracket when it's encountered.
This approach ensures that brackets are closed in the correct order, but you still need to consider the case when the stack is empty and a closing bracket is encountered.

editorial.title: Balanced Parentheses: From Brute Force to Linear Stack Scan
editorial.intuition: A string of brackets is balanced only if every opening bracket finds a matching closing one in the correct order. The naive way is to try all possible pairings, which explodes combinatorially. The key insight is that the structure is inherently hierarchical: an opening bracket must be closed before any outer bracket can be closed. This nesting property allows a simple last‑in‑first‑out mechanism—exactly what a stack provides—so we can verify balance in a single left‑to‑right pass.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute‑force approach would enumerate all ways to pair opening and closing brackets, checking each candidate for correct ordering. For a string of length n, there are O(2^n) possible pairings, leading to exponential time. Even with pruning, the algorithm remains impractical for n up to 10,000, as required by the problem constraints.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal solution uses a stack to keep track of unmatched opening brackets. As we scan the string, we push opening brackets onto the stack. When encountering a closing bracket, we pop the stack and verify that the popped opening bracket matches the closing one. If at any point the stack is empty or the brackets mismatch, the string is unbalanced. After processing all characters, the string is balanced only if the stack is empty.
editorial.optimalSolution: This stack‑based algorithm runs in O(n) time and uses O(n) space in the worst case (when all characters are opening brackets). It satisfies the problem’s constraints and is straightforward to implement.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The algorithm maintains the invariant that the stack contains exactly the sequence of unmatched opening brackets seen so far, in the order they were encountered. When a closing bracket appears, the only possible matching opening bracket is the most recent unmatched one, which is at the top of the stack. If the top does not match, no valid pairing exists, so the string is unbalanced. If all characters are processed and the stack is empty, every opening bracket has been matched correctly, proving the string is balanced.
editorial.dryRun: Consider the string "{[()]}". The stack evolves as follows: push '{', push '[', push '(', pop '(' on ')', pop '[' on ']', pop '{' on '}'. The stack is empty at the end, so the string is balanced. For an unbalanced string like "{[(])}", the algorithm will detect a mismatch when processing ']' because the top of the stack is '(', leading to an immediate failure.
commonMistakes:
Assuming that any closing bracket can match any earlier opening bracket; the order matters.
Treating the string as a simple count of brackets without respecting nesting.
Forgetting to check that the stack is empty after the entire scan.
Using recursion or backtracking, which introduces unnecessary overhead.
Ignoring the possibility of an empty input string (though the problem guarantees length ≥1).
Misinterpreting the input format and reading the length incorrectly.
editorial.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the length of the string
editorial.spaceComplexity: O(n) in the worst case for the stack
